Isaiah 63 opens with a stunning image that forces us to reckon with an uncomfortable truth about God: His salvation and His judgment are inseparable. The figure approaching from Edom is stained with blood, not from His own defeat but from treading the winepress of God's wrath alone. This is Christ in His role as judge, and the passage makes clear that redemption for God's people comes through vengeance on His enemies. When He says "the day of vengeance was in My heart, and the year of My redemption had come," these are not contradictory impulses—they are one action viewed from two angles. God's people are saved because His enemies are crushed. We often want a Jesus who saves without judging, but Scripture gives us no such option. The blood on His garments is the price of our deliverance, and we must not turn away from this reality.

Yet immediately after this terrifying vision of divine wrath, the chapter pivots to Israel's unfaithfulness and God's tender compassion. The people recall how the Angel of His Presence afflicted Himself with them in their distress, how He bore their griefs and carried them like a parent carries a child. This same God who treads nations in His fury is also the One who lifts His people up and will not abandon them—until they rebel. And they do rebel. They grieve His Holy Spirit, and He becomes their enemy, fighting against them with the same strength that conquered Edom. This is the radical nature of God's relationship with His covenant people: intimate love that becomes active opposition when rejected. There is no neutrality in His presence.

The prayer that follows is born from this memory and this judgment. Israel cries out from degradation, asking where the God who divided the Red Sea has gone, where His zeal and compassion are now. But notice the foundation of their appeal: "Yet You are our Father... our Redeemer from Everlasting is Your name." Even in exile, even under the weight of God's discipline, they do not appeal to their own righteousness or merit. They cling to God's eternal commitment to redeem. They acknowledge that He has made them stray and hardened their hearts, yet they beg Him to return—not for their sake ultimately, but "for the sake of Your servants, the tribes of Your heritage." This is how the faithful pray in judgment: not with excuses, but with the memory of God's past faithfulness and a desperate plea for Him to act for the sake of His own name and covenant.

What Isaiah 63 teaches us is that God's judgment upon our enemies and His mercy toward His people are expressions of the same holy love. His anger is not arbitrary cruelty but the inevitable response of a holy God to rebellion. And His compassion toward those who belong to Him is not weakness but the fierce devotion of a Father who will not let His children go. If you find yourself in seasons of discipline, remember that you are not abandoned but being refined. If you see God's judgment on the world, remember that it serves the redemption of His people. The stained garments of the Judge and the tender arms of the Redeemer belong to the same Christ—and that should move us to worship, to repentance, and to trust.
